---
title: "Linear Transforms of Random Vectors"
subtitle: "Core Sample · Probability Foundations"
---

```{python}
#| label: setup
#| include: false
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

# A consistent, restrained visual style for every figure in this article.
plt.rcParams.update({
    "figure.figsize": (6.2, 4.0),
    "figure.dpi": 140,
    "axes.spines.top": False,
    "axes.spines.right": False,
    "axes.grid": True,
    "grid.alpha": 0.25,
    "font.size": 11,
    "axes.titlesize": 12,
    "lines.linewidth": 2.0,
})

CLOUD  = "#2b3a67"     # the raw point cloud
MAPPED = "#c1121f"     # the transformed cloud / fitted object
ACCENT = "#0a7d4d"     # highlights / the mean vector
GREY   = "#6c757d"

def cov_ellipse_xy(mean, cov, n_std=2.0, n_pts=200):
    """Return the (x, y) coordinates of the n_std covariance ellipse of a 2-D
    Gaussian with the given mean and 2x2 covariance. Uses the eigendecomposition
    Sigma = V L V^T, so the ellipse axes point along the eigenvectors with
    half-lengths n_std * sqrt(eigenvalue) -- exactly the geometry this article
    explains. Used only to DRAW the pictures, never to derive anything."""
    vals, vecs = np.linalg.eigh(cov)            # ascending eigenvalues
    t = np.linspace(0.0, 2.0 * np.pi, n_pts)
    circle = np.stack([np.cos(t), np.sin(t)])   # unit circle, shape (2, n_pts)
    # scale each axis by n_std * sqrt(eigenvalue), then rotate by the eigenvectors
    axes_scaled = (vecs * (n_std * np.sqrt(vals))) @ circle
    return mean[0] + axes_scaled[0], mean[1] + axes_scaled[1]
```

> **Where this sits in the track.** This is the second of the two **random-vector**
> articles that close Movement 4 of the Probability Foundations track. It assumes
> [PROB-17 *Random Vectors, Mean Vectors, and Covariance Matrices*] for the three objects
> we operate on here — a random vector $\mathbf X=(X_1,\dots,X_d)$, its mean vector
> $\boldsymbol\mu=E[\mathbf X]$, and its covariance matrix $\boldsymbol\Sigma$ with
> variances on the diagonal and covariances off it. It also leans on [PROB-05
> *Expectation*] (linearity of expectation, the single workhorse identity), [PROB-06
> *Variance*] and [PROB-07 *Joint Distributions, Covariance, and Correlation*] (covariance
> and its bilinearity), and on the closure facts of [PROB-13 *The Normal Distribution*]
> when we transform the Gaussian. We use vectors and matrices *operationally* — addition,
> multiplication, transpose — and build every matrix manipulation on the page; the
> linear-algebra **theory** (eigendecomposition, positive-definiteness, matrix square
> roots) belongs to the Geometry of Data track and is flagged, not assumed, where we
> touch it. It **sets up**, by name, the single identity that downstream tracks consume:
> [REGR-06] derives the sampling covariance $\operatorname{Var}(\hat{\boldsymbol\beta}) =
> \sigma^2 (X^\top X)^{-1}$ directly from the rule proved here; [REGR-11] (generalized
> least squares) runs on the whitening transform of @sec-whitening; and the
> Geometry-of-Data and Optimization tracks reuse the quadratic form $\mathbf
> a^\top\boldsymbol\Sigma\mathbf a$.

## Abstract {.unnumbered}

Take a random vector $\mathbf X$ with mean vector $\boldsymbol\mu$ and covariance matrix
$\boldsymbol\Sigma$, hit it with a fixed matrix $A$ and a fixed shift $\mathbf b$, and ask
what happens to its first two moments. The answer is two formulas, and the whole article
is their derivation from nothing above the random-vector definitions:
$$
E[A\mathbf X + \mathbf b] = A\boldsymbol\mu + \mathbf b,
\qquad
\operatorname{Cov}(A\mathbf X + \mathbf b) = A\,\boldsymbol\Sigma\,A^\top .
$$
The single claim this article defends is that **these two lines are the engine room of
multivariate statistics**: the mean rule is just linearity of expectation wearing a
matrix coat, the covariance rule is the matrix shadow of the scalar fact
$\operatorname{Var}(aX)=a^2\operatorname{Var}(X)$ — the $A(\cdot)A^\top$ "sandwich" is
where the *square* went — and once you have them, the scalar variance of a portfolio, the
sampling distribution of a regression coefficient, the rotation of a covariance ellipse,
and the *whitening* transform that decorrelates data all fall out as special cases of one
identity.

# The Core {#sec-core}

::: {.callout-important title="The Core"}
A linear map $\mathbf X \mapsto A\mathbf X + \mathbf b$ acts on the two summaries of a
random vector in the simplest way you could hope for. The **mean vector goes through the
map unchanged in form** — $E[A\mathbf X+\mathbf b]=A\boldsymbol\mu+\mathbf b$ — because
expectation is linear and a constant shift just shifts the average. The **covariance
matrix gets sandwiched** — $\operatorname{Cov}(A\mathbf X+\mathbf b)=A\boldsymbol\Sigma
A^\top$ — with the shift $\mathbf b$ *vanishing* (sliding a cloud never changes its
spread) and the matrix $A$ appearing *twice*, once on each side, because variance is built
from a product of two deviations and each deviation gets multiplied by $A$. That second
$A^\top$ is the matrix fingerprint of the scalar "$a^2$" in
$\operatorname{Var}(aX)=a^2\operatorname{Var}(X)$.
:::

Everything below orbits that box. The sandwich $A\boldsymbol\Sigma A^\top$ will look at
first like a rule to memorize, but by the end it should feel forced: covariance is an
*expectation of a product of two centered factors*, a linear map multiplies each factor by
$A$, and a transpose appears the instant one of those factors sits on the right of the
other. Hold onto the picture of a cloud of points being stretched and rotated — that is
all $A$ does to $\boldsymbol\Sigma$, and the formula is just its bookkeeping.

# Why we are doing this: the problem that forced the formula {#sec-why}

Keep one question in view for the whole article: **when we run a random vector through a
linear map, where do its mean and its spread go, and why exactly there?** Every algebraic
step below answers a piece of that.

## The original problem: too many measurements, combined linearly

The need for these formulas is as old as least squares itself, and it comes from the same
place (the astronomy and geodesy of @sec-why in [REGR-01]). An astronomer around 1800 did
not observe one quantity; he observed a *vector* of noisy measurements — several sightings
of a planet, several angles of a survey triangle — and the quantity he actually cared
about was some **linear combination** of them: a weighted average, a difference, a
predicted position computed from a fitted orbit. Each raw measurement carried random
error, those errors were often *correlated* (the same instrument, the same atmosphere
distorts several readings together), and the practical question was unavoidable: *if I
form this linear combination of correlated noisy numbers, how noisy — and how correlated —
is the result?*

Gauss answered it for the scalar pieces, and the answer is the propagation-of-error rule
every lab course still teaches: the variance of a sum is the sum of the variances plus
twice the covariances (the identity built in [PROB-07]). What [PROB-17] gave us was a way to
*package* all those variances and covariances into one object, the covariance matrix
$\boldsymbol\Sigma$. What this article does is package the *propagation rule* to match: a
single matrix identity, $A\boldsymbol\Sigma A^\top$, that does in one line what would
otherwise be a page of bookkeeping over every pair of components. The motivation is
compression — but compression that *reveals structure*, because the matrix form makes
properties (symmetry, non-negativity, the rotation of the ellipse) visible that the
component-by-component sums hide.

::: {.callout-tip title="You might be asked"}
*"Why bother with the matrix form $A\boldsymbol\Sigma A^\top$ — isn't it the same content
as the scalar variance-of-a-sum rule from [PROB-07]?"* It is exactly the same content, and
that is the point you should make. The matrix form is not new probability; it is the
scalar rule $\operatorname{Var}(\sum_i a_i X_i)=\sum_{i}\sum_j a_i a_j
\operatorname{Cov}(X_i,X_j)$ written so that *all* the linear combinations you might form
(every row of $A$) and *all* their pairwise covariances are computed at once. The payoff is
threefold: (i) you stop re-deriving the double sum every time; (ii) the result is
automatically a valid covariance matrix (symmetric, positive semidefinite — proved in
@sec-psd); and (iii) properties of the map $A$ (rotation, scaling, projection) translate
into geometry of the spread. Interviewers ask this to check that you see the matrix
identity as *organized scalar algebra*, not as a separate magic rule.
:::

# Intuition before symbols {#sec-intuition}

Before any algebra, picture what a linear map does to a *cloud* of points. A random vector
$\mathbf X$ in two dimensions is a cloud scattered in the plane; its mean vector
$\boldsymbol\mu$ is the cloud's center of mass, and its covariance matrix $\boldsymbol\Sigma$
is encoded by an *ellipse* drawn around that center — wide in directions where the cloud
spreads, narrow where it is tight, tilted if the two coordinates are correlated.

A map $\mathbf X\mapsto A\mathbf X+\mathbf b$ does two visually separate things:

- The shift $+\mathbf b$ **slides the whole cloud** rigidly. Its center moves from
  $\boldsymbol\mu$ to $\boldsymbol\mu+\mathbf b$. Sliding changes *where* the cloud is but
  not *how it is shaped* — so $\mathbf b$ must appear in the mean and be *absent* from the
  covariance. Hold that prediction; the algebra will confirm it.
- The matrix $A$ **stretches, rotates, and shears** the cloud about the origin. It is the
  part that reshapes the ellipse. A diagonal $A$ stretches along the axes; a rotation $A$
  spins the ellipse; a general $A$ does both at once and can also flatten the cloud onto a
  line (if $A$ collapses a dimension).

@fig-cloud-map shows exactly this: the same cloud before and after a map that rotates and
stretches it, with the covariance ellipse riding along. The entire formal content of the
article is the claim that the new ellipse is the one with covariance $A\boldsymbol\Sigma
A^\top$ — and you can already *see* it is a rotated, stretched version of the old one.

```{python}
#| label: fig-cloud-map
#| fig-cap: "A linear map reshapes a cloud. **Left:** a sample from a random vector with mean $\\boldsymbol\\mu$ (green) and covariance $\\boldsymbol\\Sigma$; the dashed ellipse is the 2-standard-deviation contour of $\\boldsymbol\\Sigma$. **Right:** every point pushed through $\\mathbf x\\mapsto A\\mathbf x+\\mathbf b$. The cloud is rotated and stretched, its center slides to $A\\boldsymbol\\mu+\\mathbf b$, and the new ellipse is exactly the contour of $A\\boldsymbol\\Sigma A^\\top$ (drawn from that formula, not fit to the points). The picture *is* the theorem."
rng = np.random.default_rng(3)
mu = np.array([1.0, 0.5])
Sigma = np.array([[1.6, 0.0],
                  [0.0, 0.35]])
# draw a sample using a known factor of Sigma (sqrt of diagonal here)
L = np.array([[np.sqrt(1.6), 0.0], [0.0, np.sqrt(0.35)]])
Z = rng.normal(size=(2, 400))
X = mu[:, None] + L @ Z

theta = np.deg2rad(40.0)
A = np.array([[np.cos(theta), -np.sin(theta)],
              [np.sin(theta),  np.cos(theta)]]) @ np.array([[1.4, 0.0], [0.0, 1.0]])
b = np.array([0.5, 1.0])
Y = A @ X + b[:, None]

mu_Y = A @ mu + b
Sigma_Y = A @ Sigma @ A.T

fig, (axL, axR) = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(8.8, 4.0))
axL.scatter(X[0], X[1], s=12, color=CLOUD, alpha=0.5)
ex, ey = cov_ellipse_xy(mu, Sigma, n_std=2.0)
axL.plot(ex, ey, color=CLOUD, ls="--", lw=1.6)
axL.scatter([mu[0]], [mu[1]], color=ACCENT, s=80, edgecolor="white", zorder=5)
axL.set_title(r"Before: mean $\mu$, covariance $\Sigma$")
axL.set_xlabel("$x_1$"); axL.set_ylabel("$x_2$"); axL.set_aspect("equal")

axR.scatter(Y[0], Y[1], s=12, color=MAPPED, alpha=0.5)
ex2, ey2 = cov_ellipse_xy(mu_Y, Sigma_Y, n_std=2.0)
axR.plot(ex2, ey2, color=MAPPED, ls="--", lw=1.6)
axR.scatter([mu_Y[0]], [mu_Y[1]], color=ACCENT, s=80, edgecolor="white", zorder=5)
axR.set_title(r"After: mean $A\mu+b$, covariance $A\Sigma A^\top$")
axR.set_xlabel("$y_1$"); axR.set_ylabel("$y_2$"); axR.set_aspect("equal")
plt.tight_layout()
```

::: {.callout-tip title="You might be asked"}
*"Why does the shift $\mathbf b$ drop out of the covariance but not the mean?"* Because
the mean records *location* and the covariance records *spread about that location*.
Sliding a cloud changes its location (so $\mathbf b$ shows up in the mean) but leaves every
point's distance from the center unchanged (so $\mathbf b$ cannot affect the spread). When
we center to compute covariance, the shift cancels against itself — you will see it cancel
explicitly in @eq-b-cancels. This is the multivariate echo of $\operatorname{Var}(X+c) =
\operatorname{Var}(X)$ from [PROB-06].
:::

# The formal development {#sec-formal}

## What we are operating on: notation, fixed on the page {#sec-notation}

We work with a **random vector**, the object built in [PROB-17]. We restate every symbol so
nothing is implicit.

::: {#def-random-vector}
**Definition (random vector, mean vector, covariance matrix).** A **random vector** is a
column of $d$ random variables stacked together,
$$
\mathbf X = \begin{pmatrix} X_1 \\ X_2 \\ \vdots \\ X_d \end{pmatrix},
$$
each $X_i$ a scalar random variable on the same sample space. Its **mean vector** is the
column of component means,
$$
\boldsymbol\mu \;=\; E[\mathbf X] \;=\;
\begin{pmatrix} E[X_1] \\ \vdots \\ E[X_d] \end{pmatrix},
$$
i.e. expectation is applied **component by component**. Its **covariance matrix** is the
$d\times d$ matrix
$$
\boldsymbol\Sigma \;=\; \operatorname{Cov}(\mathbf X)
\;=\; E\!\big[(\mathbf X-\boldsymbol\mu)(\mathbf X-\boldsymbol\mu)^\top\big],
$$
whose $(i,j)$ entry is $\operatorname{Cov}(X_i,X_j)=E[(X_i-\mu_i)(X_j-\mu_j)]$ — variances
$\operatorname{Var}(X_i)$ on the diagonal ($i=j$) and covariances off it.
:::

Two pieces of that definition deserve a slow line, because the whole article rides on them.

**Why expectation is "component by component."** The expectation of a vector is *defined*
to be the vector of expectations — there is no other sensible meaning, since the only
arithmetic we have on $\mathbf X$ is done coordinate by coordinate. We will use this
together with one fact imported from linear algebra: matrix–vector multiplication produces
each output coordinate as a *sum* of input coordinates times fixed numbers. That is the
only property of matrices we need, and we state it explicitly now.

::: {#def-matrix-row}
**Definition (the one matrix fact we use).** If $A$ is an $m\times d$ matrix with entries
$A_{ki}$ and $\mathbf X$ is a $d$-vector, then $A\mathbf X$ is the $m$-vector whose $k$-th
component is
$$
(A\mathbf X)_k \;=\; \sum_{i=1}^{d} A_{ki}\,X_i .
$$
That is the *definition* of matrix–vector multiplication: each output coordinate is a
fixed-weighted sum of the input coordinates. Everything matrix-shaped below is built from
this one rule and the corresponding rule for a matrix product, $(AB)_{kj}=\sum_i
A_{ki}B_{ij}$.
:::

::: {.callout-note title="What we borrowed, and where it is built"}
We use matrices *operationally* — the multiplication rule of @def-matrix-row, the transpose
$(AB)^\top = B^\top A^\top$, and (in @sec-whitening) the eigendecomposition of a symmetric
matrix. These are notation and mechanics, and we manipulate them in full on the page. The
linear-algebra **theory** that explains *why* a symmetric matrix has an eigendecomposition,
what positive-definiteness means geometrically, and how the singular value decomposition
generalizes all this, is owned by the **Geometry of Data** track. We import the mechanics
here so the probability argument can run end to end; we do not re-derive linear algebra.
:::

The map we study is the **affine map** (a linear part plus a constant shift):
$$
\mathbf Y \;=\; A\mathbf X + \mathbf b ,
$$ {#eq-map}
where $A$ is a **fixed, non-random** $m\times d$ matrix and $\mathbf b$ is a **fixed,
non-random** $m$-vector. "Fixed" is doing heavy lifting: $A$ and $\mathbf b$ carry no
randomness, so they slide outside any expectation, exactly as a constant does in the scalar
linearity rule $E[aX+c]=aE[X]+c$. The output $\mathbf Y$ is an $m$-vector — note $m$ need
not equal $d$; a linear map can change the dimension (a row vector $A$ collapses $\mathbf X$
to a single number, the case in @sec-scalar).

## A two-minute refresher: linearity of expectation, in vector form {#sec-linearity-refresher}

The scalar tool is **linearity of expectation**, proved in [PROB-05]: for scalar random
variables and fixed numbers $a, c$,
$$
E[aX + c] = a\,E[X] + c, \qquad E[X_1 + X_2] = E[X_1] + E[X_2],
$$ {#eq-scalar-linearity}
and crucially this holds *whether or not the variables are independent* — it is a property
of summation, not of independence. We need exactly the second form, additivity, lifted to
vectors. Because vector expectation is component-wise (@def-random-vector), additivity is
inherited coordinate by coordinate: if $\mathbf U$ and $\mathbf V$ are random vectors of the
same length, then the $k$-th component of $E[\mathbf U+\mathbf V]$ is
$E[U_k+V_k]=E[U_k]+E[V_k]$ by @eq-scalar-linearity, which is the $k$-th component of
$E[\mathbf U]+E[\mathbf V]$. Since this holds for every $k$,
$$
E[\mathbf U + \mathbf V] = E[\mathbf U] + E[\mathbf V].
$$ {#eq-vector-additivity}
That is the only expectation property the mean rule needs.

## The mean rule: $E[A\mathbf X + \mathbf b]=A\boldsymbol\mu+\mathbf b$ {#sec-mean-rule}

::: {#thm-mean}
**Theorem (mean of a linear transform).** For a random vector $\mathbf X$ with
$E[\mathbf X]=\boldsymbol\mu$, a fixed $m\times d$ matrix $A$, and a fixed $m$-vector
$\mathbf b$,
$$
E[A\mathbf X + \mathbf b] \;=\; A\boldsymbol\mu + \mathbf b .
$$ {#eq-mean-rule}
:::

::: {.proof}
We prove it one output coordinate at a time, using only @def-matrix-row and
@eq-scalar-linearity, then reassemble. Fix an output index $k\in\{1,\dots,m\}$. By
@def-matrix-row the $k$-th component of $A\mathbf X+\mathbf b$ is
$$
(A\mathbf X + \mathbf b)_k \;=\; \sum_{i=1}^{d} A_{ki} X_i \;+\; b_k .
$$
Take the (scalar) expectation of this single coordinate. Expectation is linear
(@eq-scalar-linearity): it passes through the finite sum, the fixed numbers $A_{ki}$ pull
out front, and the fixed constant $b_k$ comes straight through:
$$
E\!\left[\sum_{i=1}^{d} A_{ki} X_i + b_k\right]
= \sum_{i=1}^{d} A_{ki}\,E[X_i] + b_k
= \sum_{i=1}^{d} A_{ki}\,\mu_i + b_k .
$$
Here we used $E[X_i]=\mu_i$, the $i$-th component of $\boldsymbol\mu$ from
@def-random-vector. Now read the right-hand side: $\sum_i A_{ki}\mu_i$ is, by
@def-matrix-row applied to the vector $\boldsymbol\mu$, exactly $(A\boldsymbol\mu)_k$. So
$$
\big(E[A\mathbf X + \mathbf b]\big)_k = (A\boldsymbol\mu)_k + b_k = (A\boldsymbol\mu + \mathbf b)_k .
$$
This holds for every coordinate $k$, and two vectors equal in every coordinate are the same
vector. Therefore $E[A\mathbf X+\mathbf b]=A\boldsymbol\mu+\mathbf b$. $\qquad\blacksquare$
:::

That is the entire mean rule: linearity of expectation, applied in each coordinate, then
repackaged. Notice we never assumed anything about the components of $\mathbf X$ — no
independence, no particular distribution. The mean rule is as universal as linearity of
expectation itself, which is to say *always true*.

::: {.callout-tip title="You might be asked"}
*"Does the mean rule need the components of $\mathbf X$ to be independent, or normal, or
anything?"* No. It is pure linearity of expectation, and linearity holds for any random
variables whose means exist — correlated, dependent, any distribution. The only requirement
is that the means $\mu_i$ are finite (an integral or sum that converges; [PROB-05] shows a
heavy-tailed case where it does not). This unconditional universality is exactly why the
mean rule is so heavily used and so rarely worried about.
:::

## A refresher on covariance, and the matrix form we will need {#sec-cov-refresher}

The covariance rule needs more care, so we set up the centered vector first. Define the
**centered** random vector
$$
\tilde{\mathbf X} \;=\; \mathbf X - \boldsymbol\mu ,
$$ {#eq-centered}
the deviation of $\mathbf X$ from its mean. By @def-random-vector the covariance matrix is
$\boldsymbol\Sigma = E[\tilde{\mathbf X}\,\tilde{\mathbf X}^\top]$. We must be completely
explicit about what the **outer product** $\tilde{\mathbf X}\,\tilde{\mathbf X}^\top$ is,
because the whole sandwich comes from it. A column $d$-vector times a row $d$-vector is a
$d\times d$ matrix whose $(i,j)$ entry is the product of the $i$-th entry of the column and
the $j$-th entry of the row:
$$
\big(\tilde{\mathbf X}\,\tilde{\mathbf X}^\top\big)_{ij}
= \tilde X_i\,\tilde X_j = (X_i-\mu_i)(X_j-\mu_j).
$$
Taking expectations entry by entry,
$$
\boldsymbol\Sigma_{ij}
= E[(X_i-\mu_i)(X_j-\mu_j)] = \operatorname{Cov}(X_i,X_j),
$$ {#eq-Sigma-entries}
which recovers exactly the definition. The reason we write covariance as the expectation of
an *outer product* — rather than as a table of pairwise covariances — is that the outer
product is what plays nicely with matrix multiplication: when we hit $\tilde{\mathbf X}$
with $A$ on the left, the transpose in $\tilde{\mathbf X}^\top$ forces an $A^\top$ on the
right, and that is the origin of the sandwich. We make that precise now.

We need one more mechanical fact, the **transpose of a product**, which we state and use but
do not re-derive (it is linear-algebra mechanics, owned by Geometry of Data): for any
conformable matrices,
$$
(A\,\mathbf v)^\top = \mathbf v^\top A^\top, \qquad (AB)^\top = B^\top A^\top .
$$ {#eq-transpose-rule}
The intuition is that transposing reverses the order and flips each factor — the same way
$(ab)^{-1}=b^{-1}a^{-1}$ reverses order for inverses.

We also need that a fixed matrix slides out of an expectation. This is the vector version of
"$E[aX]=aE[X]$," and we justify it rather than assert it.

::: {#lem-pull-out}
**Lemma (a constant matrix pulls out of expectation).** If $A$ is a fixed $m\times d$
matrix and $\mathbf X$ is a random $d$-vector with finite means, then
$$
E[A\mathbf X] = A\,E[\mathbf X],
$$
and likewise for a fixed matrix multiplying on the right of a random matrix:
$E[\mathbf Z\,C]=E[\mathbf Z]\,C$ for fixed $C$.
:::

::: {.proof}
The left case is the linear part of @thm-mean (set $\mathbf b=\mathbf 0$): coordinate $k$
of $A\mathbf X$ is $\sum_i A_{ki}X_i$, whose expectation is $\sum_i A_{ki}E[X_i]$ by scalar
linearity, which is coordinate $k$ of $A\,E[\mathbf X]$. For the right case, let $\mathbf Z$
be a random matrix with $(k,\ell)$ entry $Z_{k\ell}$ and $C$ fixed. The $(k,j)$ entry of
$\mathbf Z C$ is $\sum_\ell Z_{k\ell}C_{\ell j}$; its expectation is $\sum_\ell
E[Z_{k\ell}]C_{\ell j}$ by scalar linearity (the $C_{\ell j}$ are fixed), which is the
$(k,j)$ entry of $E[\mathbf Z]\,C$. Equal in every entry, hence equal. $\qquad\blacksquare$
:::

## The covariance rule: $\operatorname{Cov}(A\mathbf X + \mathbf b)=A\boldsymbol\Sigma A^\top$ {#sec-cov-rule}

This is the headline result — the single identity [REGR-06] consumes by name. We build it
slowly.

::: {#thm-cov}
**Theorem (covariance of a linear transform).** For a random vector $\mathbf X$ with
covariance matrix $\boldsymbol\Sigma$, a fixed $m\times d$ matrix $A$, and a fixed
$m$-vector $\mathbf b$,
$$
\operatorname{Cov}(A\mathbf X + \mathbf b) \;=\; A\,\boldsymbol\Sigma\,A^\top .
$$ {#eq-cov-rule}
In particular the shift $\mathbf b$ does not appear.
:::

::: {.proof}
Write $\mathbf Y = A\mathbf X+\mathbf b$. By the definition of a covariance matrix
(@def-random-vector) applied to $\mathbf Y$,
$$
\operatorname{Cov}(\mathbf Y)
= E\!\big[(\mathbf Y - E[\mathbf Y])(\mathbf Y - E[\mathbf Y])^\top\big].
$$
So we need the *centered* version of $\mathbf Y$. We already know its mean: by @thm-mean,
$E[\mathbf Y]=A\boldsymbol\mu+\mathbf b$. Subtract it from $\mathbf Y=A\mathbf X+\mathbf b$:
$$
\mathbf Y - E[\mathbf Y]
= (A\mathbf X + \mathbf b) - (A\boldsymbol\mu + \mathbf b)
= A\mathbf X - A\boldsymbol\mu + \underbrace{\mathbf b - \mathbf b}_{=\,\mathbf 0}
= A(\mathbf X - \boldsymbol\mu) = A\,\tilde{\mathbf X}.
$$ {#eq-b-cancels}
There is the promised cancellation: **the shift $\mathbf b$ subtracts against the $\mathbf
b$ inside its own mean and is gone.** Centering kills constants — the multivariate echo of
$\operatorname{Var}(X+c)=\operatorname{Var}(X)$. What remains is the centered input
$\tilde{\mathbf X}=\mathbf X-\boldsymbol\mu$ from @eq-centered, multiplied by $A$.

Now substitute $\mathbf Y - E[\mathbf Y]=A\tilde{\mathbf X}$ into the covariance definition.
The transpose of the second factor is $(A\tilde{\mathbf X})^\top=\tilde{\mathbf X}^\top
A^\top$ by the transpose-of-a-product rule @eq-transpose-rule:
$$
\operatorname{Cov}(\mathbf Y)
= E\!\big[(A\tilde{\mathbf X})(A\tilde{\mathbf X})^\top\big]
= E\!\big[A\,\tilde{\mathbf X}\,\tilde{\mathbf X}^\top A^\top\big].
$$
Inside the expectation sits $A\,(\tilde{\mathbf X}\tilde{\mathbf X}^\top)\,A^\top$: a fixed
matrix $A$ on the *left*, a random matrix $\tilde{\mathbf X}\tilde{\mathbf X}^\top$ in the
*middle*, a fixed matrix $A^\top$ on the *right*. By @lem-pull-out, fixed matrices slide out
of the expectation on whichever side they sit — $A$ pulls out left, $A^\top$ pulls out
right:
$$
\operatorname{Cov}(\mathbf Y)
= A\;E\!\big[\tilde{\mathbf X}\,\tilde{\mathbf X}^\top\big]\;A^\top .
$$
The middle expectation is, by @def-random-vector and @eq-Sigma-entries, precisely the
covariance matrix $\boldsymbol\Sigma$ of $\mathbf X$. Therefore
$$
\operatorname{Cov}(A\mathbf X + \mathbf b) = A\,\boldsymbol\Sigma\,A^\top . \qquad\blacksquare
$$
:::

Read the proof back for *where each piece of the formula came from*, because that is what
makes it memorable rather than memorized:

- **The $A$ on the left** is the $A$ that multiplied $\mathbf X$ in the map.
- **The $A^\top$ on the right** is forced by the transpose in the covariance definition: a
  covariance is an outer product $(\cdot)(\cdot)^\top$, and transposing $A\tilde{\mathbf
  X}$ flips $A$ to $A^\top$ and sends it to the other side (@eq-transpose-rule).
- **The shift $\mathbf b$ is absent** because it cancelled in @eq-b-cancels when we
  centered.

So the sandwich is not arbitrary. The two $A$'s are the two centered factors of a
covariance, each carrying its own $A$, with a transpose between them.

::: {.callout-tip title="You might be asked"}
*"Where did the 'square' in $\operatorname{Var}(aX)=a^2\operatorname{Var}(X)$ go in the
matrix version?"* It became the *two separate appearances* of $A$, one on each side. In the
scalar case both factors of the covariance carry the same number $a$, so they collapse into
$a\cdot a=a^2$. In the matrix case the two factors carry $A$ and $A^\top$ (the transpose
appearing because of the outer-product structure), and they cannot collapse into a single
"$A^2$" because $A\boldsymbol\Sigma A^\top \neq A^2\boldsymbol\Sigma$ in general. So the
matrix generalization of "$a^2$" is the sandwich $A(\cdot)A^\top$, and recognizing that is
the single most useful thing to carry out of this article. We confirm the exact scalar
reduction next in @sec-scalar.
:::

## Recovering the scalar special cases {#sec-scalar}

A new formula earns trust by reproducing the ones you already know. Two checks.

**One row of $A$: the variance of a linear combination.** Take $A$ to be a single row
$\mathbf a^\top=(a_1,\dots,a_d)$ and $\mathbf b=0$, so $\mathbf Y$ is the *scalar*
$\mathbf a^\top\mathbf X=\sum_i a_i X_i$ — a weighted combination of the components. The
covariance rule @eq-cov-rule gives a $1\times 1$ matrix, i.e. an ordinary number:
$$
\operatorname{Var}(\mathbf a^\top\mathbf X) = \mathbf a^\top\boldsymbol\Sigma\,\mathbf a .
$$ {#eq-quadratic-form}
Let us confirm this *is* the variance-of-a-sum rule from [PROB-07], not a new claim. Expand
the quadratic form using @def-matrix-row twice (once for $\boldsymbol\Sigma\mathbf a$, once
for the outer dot product):
$$
\mathbf a^\top\boldsymbol\Sigma\,\mathbf a
= \sum_{i=1}^d\sum_{j=1}^d a_i\,\boldsymbol\Sigma_{ij}\,a_j
= \sum_{i=1}^d\sum_{j=1}^d a_i a_j\operatorname{Cov}(X_i,X_j) ,
$$
using @eq-Sigma-entries for $\boldsymbol\Sigma_{ij}$. Split the double sum into its
diagonal ($i=j$, where $\operatorname{Cov}(X_i,X_i)=\operatorname{Var}(X_i)$) and
off-diagonal terms, and note the off-diagonal terms pair up symmetrically
($\operatorname{Cov}(X_i,X_j)=\operatorname{Cov}(X_j,X_i)$, so the $(i,j)$ and $(j,i)$
terms are equal):
$$
\mathbf a^\top\boldsymbol\Sigma\,\mathbf a
= \sum_{i=1}^d a_i^2\operatorname{Var}(X_i)
\;+\; 2\!\!\sum_{1\le i<j\le d}\!\! a_i a_j\operatorname{Cov}(X_i,X_j) .
$$
For $d=2$ this is exactly $\operatorname{Var}(a_1X_1+a_2X_2)=a_1^2\operatorname{Var}(X_1)
+a_2^2\operatorname{Var}(X_2)+2a_1a_2\operatorname{Cov}(X_1,X_2)$, the identity proved by
hand in [PROB-07]. So @eq-quadratic-form is the variance-of-a-sum rule, *for every number of
terms at once*. This is the form the **Optimization** track (quadratic forms) and
portfolio theory (@sec-portfolio) consume directly.

**A diagonal $A$ with one entry: the scalar $a^2$ rule.** Take $d=m=1$, so $A=(a)$ is a
single number, $\boldsymbol\Sigma=(\sigma^2)$, and @eq-cov-rule reads $a\,\sigma^2\,a^\top =
a\sigma^2 a = a^2\sigma^2$ — the transpose of a number is itself, the two $A$'s collapse to
$a^2$, and we recover $\operatorname{Var}(aX)=a^2\operatorname{Var}(X)$ exactly. The matrix
rule contains the scalar rule as its smallest case.

## The covariance rule guarantees a *valid* covariance matrix {#sec-psd}

A covariance matrix cannot be just any matrix. From its definition it must be **symmetric**
($\operatorname{Cov}(X_i,X_j)=\operatorname{Cov}(X_j,X_i)$) and **positive semidefinite**
($\mathbf a^\top\boldsymbol\Sigma\mathbf a\ge 0$ for every $\mathbf a$, because that
quantity is a variance and variances cannot be negative — exactly @eq-quadratic-form). A
healthy worry: does the output $A\boldsymbol\Sigma A^\top$ of @eq-cov-rule still satisfy
these? If it did not, the formula would be producing nonsense matrices. It does, and the
proof is short and worth seeing.

::: {#lem-psd}
**Lemma (closure under the sandwich).** If $\boldsymbol\Sigma$ is symmetric positive
semidefinite, then so is $A\boldsymbol\Sigma A^\top$ for any conformable $A$.
:::

::: {.proof}
**Symmetry.** Transpose the product using @eq-transpose-rule (reverse order, flip each
factor) and use that $\boldsymbol\Sigma^\top=\boldsymbol\Sigma$ (given) and
$(A^\top)^\top=A$:
$$
(A\boldsymbol\Sigma A^\top)^\top
= (A^\top)^\top\,\boldsymbol\Sigma^\top\,A^\top
= A\,\boldsymbol\Sigma\,A^\top .
$$
A matrix equal to its own transpose is symmetric. **Positive semidefiniteness.** Take any
vector $\mathbf c$ (of the output dimension $m$) and form the quadratic form, grouping the
factors:
$$
\mathbf c^\top (A\boldsymbol\Sigma A^\top)\,\mathbf c
= (A^\top\mathbf c)^\top\,\boldsymbol\Sigma\,(A^\top\mathbf c)
= \mathbf w^\top\boldsymbol\Sigma\,\mathbf w \ge 0,
$$
where $\mathbf w = A^\top\mathbf c$ and the final inequality is the positive
semidefiniteness of $\boldsymbol\Sigma$ itself (applied to the vector $\mathbf w$). Since
this holds for every $\mathbf c$, the matrix $A\boldsymbol\Sigma A^\top$ is positive
semidefinite. $\qquad\blacksquare$
:::

This is reassuring and also conceptually important: it says the *set of valid covariance
matrices is closed under linear maps*. You can never accidentally leave the world of
legitimate spreads by applying a matrix — which is exactly what you want, since
$A\boldsymbol\Sigma A^\top$ is supposed to be the spread of a genuine random vector
$A\mathbf X+\mathbf b$.

::: {.callout-tip title="You might be asked"}
*"When is $A\boldsymbol\Sigma A^\top$ positive *definite* (invertible), not merely
semidefinite?"* When $A$ has full row rank and $\boldsymbol\Sigma$ is positive definite,
because then $\mathbf w=A^\top\mathbf c=\mathbf 0$ forces $\mathbf c=\mathbf 0$, so the
quadratic form is strictly positive for $\mathbf c\neq\mathbf 0$. It degenerates to merely
semidefinite (singular) the moment $A$ collapses a dimension — e.g. if a row of $A$ is a
copy of another, two outputs are perfectly correlated and the output ellipse flattens onto a
lower-dimensional subspace. That flattening is the geometric meaning of a singular
covariance, and it is the multivariate face of the degenerate $S_{xx}=0$ boundary in
[REGR-01].
:::

# The geometry: how a linear map moves the covariance ellipse {#sec-geometry}

The algebra is settled; now we *see* it. A covariance matrix is pictured by its **ellipse
of constant spread** — for a $2$-D vector, the set of points at a fixed "statistical
distance" from the mean. The shape of that ellipse is set by $\boldsymbol\Sigma$: its axes
point along the eigenvectors of $\boldsymbol\Sigma$ and their half-lengths are proportional
to the square roots of the eigenvalues (the eigendecomposition is linear-algebra theory,
flagged to Geometry of Data; here we use it only to *draw*). The covariance rule
@eq-cov-rule says the map $A$ transforms this ellipse into the ellipse of
$A\boldsymbol\Sigma A^\top$. @fig-ellipse-decomp pulls a general $A$ apart into its three
elementary actions so you can watch each one reshape the ellipse.

```{python}
#| label: fig-ellipse-decomp
#| fig-cap: "How a linear map reshapes the covariance ellipse, built up one action at a time, all drawn from $A\\Sigma A^\\top$. **(a)** the original isotropic spread $\\Sigma=I$ (a circle). **(b)** a diagonal $A=\\operatorname{diag}(2,0.7)$ stretches it into an axis-aligned ellipse. **(c)** a rotation $A=R(35^\\circ)$ spins that ellipse, introducing correlation (the tilt). **(d)** a shear $A=\\bigl[\\begin{smallmatrix}1&0.8\\\\0&1\\end{smallmatrix}\\bigr]$ slants it. In every panel the ellipse is computed from the formula, never fit to data."
def R(deg):
    t = np.deg2rad(deg)
    return np.array([[np.cos(t), -np.sin(t)], [np.sin(t), np.cos(t)]])

I2 = np.eye(2)
configs = [
    ("(a) original  $\\Sigma=I$", I2),
    ("(b) stretch  $A=\\mathrm{diag}(2,0.7)$", np.array([[2.0, 0.0], [0.0, 0.7]])),
    ("(c) rotate  $A=R(35^\\circ)$", R(35.0) @ np.array([[2.0, 0.0], [0.0, 0.7]])),
    ("(d) shear", np.array([[1.0, 0.8], [0.0, 1.0]]) @ R(35.0) @ np.array([[2.0, 0.0], [0.0, 0.7]])),
]
fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 4, figsize=(11.0, 3.0))
origin = np.zeros(2)
for ax, (title, A) in zip(axes, configs):
    Sig = A @ I2 @ A.T
    ex, ey = cov_ellipse_xy(origin, Sig, n_std=2.0)
    ax.plot(ex, ey, color=MAPPED, lw=2.0)
    # draw the eigen-axes to show the principal directions
    vals, vecs = np.linalg.eigh(Sig)
    for k in range(2):
        v = vecs[:, k] * 2.0 * np.sqrt(vals[k])
        ax.plot([0, v[0]], [0, v[1]], color=CLOUD, lw=1.4)
    ax.set_title(title, fontsize=9)
    ax.set_xlim(-5, 5); ax.set_ylim(-5, 5); ax.set_aspect("equal")
    ax.axhline(0, color=GREY, lw=0.6); ax.axvline(0, color=GREY, lw=0.6)
plt.tight_layout()
```

The figure carries the conceptual headline of the article: **the covariance matrix and the
ellipse are the same information, and the matrix $A$ does to the ellipse exactly what it
does to any shape — stretch, rotate, shear.** Off-diagonal covariance is *tilt*. A
correlation appears precisely when the ellipse is not aligned with the axes, and that
tilt is introduced by the rotational part of $A$. This is why, in @sec-whitening, we will
be able to *remove* correlation by applying the right $A$: undo the tilt and the stretch,
and the ellipse becomes a circle — a covariance matrix equal to the identity.

# The Gaussian is closed under linear maps {#sec-gaussian-closure}

So far we have tracked only the mean and the covariance — the first two moments — and for a
general $\mathbf X$ those two summaries do *not* pin down the whole distribution. There is
one family where they do, and where linear maps are especially clean: the **multivariate
normal**. The bell curve introduced in [PROB-13], lifted to vectors in [PROB-17], has the
remarkable property that a linear map of a Gaussian is *again* a Gaussian — so for it the
two formulas of this article describe the entire transformed distribution, not just its
first two moments.

::: {#thm-gaussian-closure}
**Theorem (closure of the multivariate normal under affine maps).** If $\mathbf X \sim
\mathcal N(\boldsymbol\mu,\boldsymbol\Sigma)$ (multivariate normal with mean vector
$\boldsymbol\mu$ and covariance $\boldsymbol\Sigma$), $A$ is a fixed $m\times d$ matrix, and
$\mathbf b$ a fixed $m$-vector, then
$$
A\mathbf X + \mathbf b \;\sim\; \mathcal N\!\big(A\boldsymbol\mu + \mathbf b,\; A\boldsymbol\Sigma A^\top\big).
$$
The mean and covariance are forced by @thm-mean and @thm-cov; the content of *this* theorem
is that the result is still **normal**.
:::

We do not give the full density-level proof here — it rests on the moment generating
function / characteristic function machinery that is the subject of [PROB-20], which owns
the transform tools and *will* prove this closure cleanly as one of its showcase results.
But we can give the complete logical chain so the claim is not left dangling, and flag the
one imported ingredient.

::: {.proof}
**(Chain, with the transform fact imported from [PROB-20].)** The defining property of the
multivariate normal that [PROB-20] establishes is its characteristic function: $\mathbf X
\sim\mathcal N(\boldsymbol\mu,\boldsymbol\Sigma)$ if and only if, for every fixed vector
$\mathbf t$,
$$
E\!\big[e^{\,i\,\mathbf t^\top \mathbf X}\big]
= \exp\!\Big(i\,\mathbf t^\top\boldsymbol\mu - \tfrac12\,\mathbf t^\top\boldsymbol\Sigma\,\mathbf t\Big).
$$
(This single formula encodes the entire distribution; [PROB-20] derives it and proves that
the characteristic function determines the distribution uniquely — the one fact we import.)
Now compute the characteristic function of $\mathbf Y=A\mathbf X+\mathbf b$ at an arbitrary
$\mathbf s$, moving the fixed shift out and regrouping the exponent with the transpose rule
@eq-transpose-rule:
$$
E\!\big[e^{\,i\,\mathbf s^\top \mathbf Y}\big]
= E\!\big[e^{\,i\,\mathbf s^\top (A\mathbf X + \mathbf b)}\big]
= e^{\,i\,\mathbf s^\top\mathbf b}\;E\!\big[e^{\,i\,(A^\top\mathbf s)^\top \mathbf X}\big].
$$
The remaining expectation is the characteristic function of $\mathbf X$ evaluated at the
vector $\mathbf t = A^\top\mathbf s$. Substitute it:
$$
= e^{\,i\,\mathbf s^\top\mathbf b}\;
\exp\!\Big(i\,(A^\top\mathbf s)^\top\boldsymbol\mu
 - \tfrac12\,(A^\top\mathbf s)^\top\boldsymbol\Sigma\,(A^\top\mathbf s)\Big).
$$
Simplify each piece with $(A^\top\mathbf s)^\top = \mathbf s^\top A$: the linear part of the
exponent is $i\,\mathbf s^\top\mathbf b + i\,\mathbf s^\top A\boldsymbol\mu = i\,\mathbf
s^\top(A\boldsymbol\mu+\mathbf b)$, and the quadratic part is $-\tfrac12\,\mathbf
s^\top(A\boldsymbol\Sigma A^\top)\mathbf s$. Hence
$$
E\!\big[e^{\,i\,\mathbf s^\top \mathbf Y}\big]
= \exp\!\Big(i\,\mathbf s^\top(A\boldsymbol\mu+\mathbf b)
 - \tfrac12\,\mathbf s^\top (A\boldsymbol\Sigma A^\top)\,\mathbf s\Big).
$$
This is *exactly* the characteristic function of a $\mathcal N(A\boldsymbol\mu+\mathbf b,\,
A\boldsymbol\Sigma A^\top)$ distribution. Because the characteristic function determines the
distribution uniquely (the imported [PROB-20] fact), $\mathbf Y$ has that distribution.
$\qquad\blacksquare$
:::

@fig-gaussian-closure makes the closure visible: a Gaussian cloud mapped by $A$ stays a
Gaussian cloud (elliptical, with a single mode), and even projecting it down to one
dimension yields a univariate bell curve whose mean and variance match
$A\boldsymbol\mu+\mathbf b$ and $A\boldsymbol\Sigma A^\top$.

```{python}
#| label: fig-gaussian-closure
#| fig-cap: "Closure of the Gaussian under a linear map. **Left:** a sample from $\\mathbf X\\sim\\mathcal N(\\boldsymbol\\mu,\\boldsymbol\\Sigma)$ with its 2-SD ellipse. **Middle:** $\\mathbf Y=A\\mathbf X$ is still elliptical and unimodal -- still Gaussian -- with ellipse $A\\Sigma A^\\top$. **Right:** the linear combination $w=\\mathbf a^\\top\\mathbf X$ (a 1-D projection) is a univariate normal; the curve is the $\\mathcal N(\\mathbf a^\\top\\boldsymbol\\mu,\\,\\mathbf a^\\top\\Sigma\\mathbf a)$ density predicted by the rules, overlaid on the histogram of the projected sample."
rng = np.random.default_rng(11)
mu = np.array([0.0, 0.0])
Sigma = np.array([[2.0, 1.1], [1.1, 1.0]])
# a genuine matrix square root via eigendecomposition, to SAMPLE a known Gaussian
vals, vecs = np.linalg.eigh(Sigma)
Lroot = vecs @ np.diag(np.sqrt(vals)) @ vecs.T
Z = rng.normal(size=(2, 3000))
X = mu[:, None] + Lroot @ Z

A = np.array([[1.0, -0.6], [0.4, 1.2]])
Y = A @ X
Sigma_Y = A @ Sigma @ A.T

a = np.array([1.0, -1.0])               # projection direction
w = a @ X
mean_w = a @ mu
var_w = a @ Sigma @ a

fig, (ax1, ax2, ax3) = plt.subplots(1, 3, figsize=(11.2, 3.4))
ax1.scatter(X[0], X[1], s=6, color=CLOUD, alpha=0.4)
ex, ey = cov_ellipse_xy(mu, Sigma, 2.0)
ax1.plot(ex, ey, color=CLOUD, lw=1.8)
ax1.set_title(r"$\mathbf X\sim\mathcal N(\mu,\Sigma)$"); ax1.set_aspect("equal")
ax1.set_xlabel("$x_1$"); ax1.set_ylabel("$x_2$")

ax2.scatter(Y[0], Y[1], s=6, color=MAPPED, alpha=0.4)
ex2, ey2 = cov_ellipse_xy(A @ mu, Sigma_Y, 2.0)
ax2.plot(ex2, ey2, color=MAPPED, lw=1.8)
ax2.set_title(r"$\mathbf Y=A\mathbf X$ (still Gaussian)"); ax2.set_aspect("equal")
ax2.set_xlabel("$y_1$"); ax2.set_ylabel("$y_2$")

ax3.hist(w, bins=40, density=True, color=GREY, alpha=0.55)
grid = np.linspace(w.min(), w.max(), 200)
dens = np.exp(-0.5 * (grid - mean_w) ** 2 / var_w) / np.sqrt(2 * np.pi * var_w)
ax3.plot(grid, dens, color=ACCENT, lw=2.2,
         label=fr"$\mathcal N({mean_w:.2f},\,{var_w:.2f})$")
ax3.set_title(r"$w=\mathbf a^\top\mathbf X$ (univariate normal)")
ax3.set_xlabel("$w$"); ax3.legend(fontsize=9, framealpha=0.9)
plt.tight_layout()
```

::: {.callout-warning title="Pitfall"}
**Closure is a property of the *normal* family, not of linear maps in general.** The mean
rule @eq-mean-rule and covariance rule @eq-cov-rule hold for *any* random vector with finite
moments — but the *distributional* closure of @thm-gaussian-closure holds only because the
Gaussian is determined by its first two moments. For a non-Gaussian $\mathbf X$, $A\mathbf
X+\mathbf b$ has mean $A\boldsymbol\mu+\mathbf b$ and covariance $A\boldsymbol\Sigma A^\top$
(those are always right), but its *shape* can change completely: a sum of independent
uniforms is not uniform, it bends toward a bell (that bending, taken to the limit, is the
Central Limit Theorem, owned by the Inference track). Do not silently assume the output is
the same distributional family as the input unless you started Gaussian.
:::

# The whitening transform: using the formula to *manufacture* a covariance {#sec-whitening}

So far we have *computed* the covariance of an output. Now we run the formula in reverse:
*choose* $A$ to make the output covariance be whatever we want — specifically, the identity.
This is **whitening** (or sphering), and it is the single most useful application of
@eq-cov-rule. It is the engine of generalized least squares ([REGR-11]) and of the
preprocessing step that lets correlated features be treated as if they were independent.

The goal: given $\mathbf X$ with covariance $\boldsymbol\Sigma$ (assume positive definite,
so it is invertible and has an invertible square root), find a matrix $W$ so that $\mathbf
Z = W\mathbf X$ has $\operatorname{Cov}(\mathbf Z)=I$ — uncorrelated components, each of unit
variance. By the covariance rule @eq-cov-rule, $\operatorname{Cov}(\mathbf Z)=W\boldsymbol
\Sigma W^\top$, so we need
$$
W\,\boldsymbol\Sigma\,W^\top = I .
$$ {#eq-whitening-goal}
We must produce a $W$ solving this. We use one imported tool, the **symmetric matrix square
root**, and we are explicit about what it is and that it is imported.

::: {.callout-note title="The one imported ingredient"}
Because $\boldsymbol\Sigma$ is symmetric and positive definite, linear algebra (owned by
**Geometry of Data**) guarantees it can be written $\boldsymbol\Sigma = V\Lambda V^\top$
with $V$ orthogonal ($V^\top V = I$) and $\Lambda$ diagonal with **positive** entries (the
eigenvalues). From this one defines the **inverse square root**
$$
\boldsymbol\Sigma^{-1/2} := V\,\Lambda^{-1/2}\,V^\top,
\qquad \Lambda^{-1/2} = \operatorname{diag}\!\big(\lambda_1^{-1/2},\dots,\lambda_d^{-1/2}\big),
$$
which is itself symmetric ($(\boldsymbol\Sigma^{-1/2})^\top = \boldsymbol\Sigma^{-1/2}$,
since $V\Lambda^{-1/2}V^\top$ transposed is itself) and satisfies
$\boldsymbol\Sigma^{-1/2}\boldsymbol\Sigma\,\boldsymbol\Sigma^{-1/2} = I$. We import the
*existence* of this decomposition; we verify the whitening identity from it ourselves
below.
:::

Take $W = \boldsymbol\Sigma^{-1/2}$. We check it whitens, using only @eq-cov-rule, the
symmetry of $\boldsymbol\Sigma^{-1/2}$, and the property
$\boldsymbol\Sigma^{-1/2}\boldsymbol\Sigma\,\boldsymbol\Sigma^{-1/2}=I$ just stated:
$$
\operatorname{Cov}(\boldsymbol\Sigma^{-1/2}\mathbf X)
= \boldsymbol\Sigma^{-1/2}\,\boldsymbol\Sigma\,(\boldsymbol\Sigma^{-1/2})^\top
= \boldsymbol\Sigma^{-1/2}\,\boldsymbol\Sigma\,\boldsymbol\Sigma^{-1/2}
= I .
$$
The middle step replaced $(\boldsymbol\Sigma^{-1/2})^\top$ with
$\boldsymbol\Sigma^{-1/2}$ (symmetry), and the last step is the defining property of the
inverse square root. So
$$
\boxed{\;\mathbf Z = \boldsymbol\Sigma^{-1/2}\,(\mathbf X - \boldsymbol\mu)
\quad\text{has}\quad E[\mathbf Z]=\mathbf 0,\;\;\operatorname{Cov}(\mathbf Z)=I.\;}
$$ {#eq-whitening}
(We also subtracted $\boldsymbol\mu$ to center, using the mean rule to send the mean to
$\boldsymbol\Sigma^{-1/2}(\boldsymbol\mu-\boldsymbol\mu)=\mathbf 0$.) The transformed vector
$\mathbf Z$ is "white": zero mean, unit variance in every direction, **no correlation
between any pair of components** — a perfectly round cloud. @fig-whitening shows a
correlated cloud being whitened into a circular one.

```{python}
#| label: fig-whitening
#| fig-cap: "Whitening in action. **Left:** a strongly correlated cloud, $\\boldsymbol\\Sigma$ has a tilted, elongated ellipse. **Right:** after $\\mathbf Z=\\boldsymbol\\Sigma^{-1/2}(\\mathbf X-\\boldsymbol\\mu)$ the cloud is a unit circle -- covariance $I$, drawn from the formula. Whitening *undoes* the stretch and tilt that $\\boldsymbol\\Sigma$ encodes; it is the inverse of the reshaping in @fig-ellipse-decomp. Generalized least squares ([REGR-11]) applies exactly this map to correlated regression errors so that ordinary least squares becomes valid again."
rng = np.random.default_rng(7)
mu = np.array([2.0, 1.0])
Sigma = np.array([[3.0, 2.1], [2.1, 1.8]])
vals, vecs = np.linalg.eigh(Sigma)
Lroot = vecs @ np.diag(np.sqrt(vals)) @ vecs.T            # a square root, to sample
Z0 = rng.normal(size=(2, 1500))
X = mu[:, None] + Lroot @ Z0

Sig_inv_sqrt = vecs @ np.diag(vals ** -0.5) @ vecs.T       # the whitening matrix
Zw = Sig_inv_sqrt @ (X - mu[:, None])
Sigma_Z = Sig_inv_sqrt @ Sigma @ Sig_inv_sqrt.T            # should be ~ I

fig, (axL, axR) = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(8.8, 4.0))
axL.scatter(X[0], X[1], s=8, color=CLOUD, alpha=0.4)
ex, ey = cov_ellipse_xy(mu, Sigma, 2.0)
axL.plot(ex, ey, color=CLOUD, lw=1.8)
axL.set_title(r"Correlated: $\Sigma$ tilted ellipse"); axL.set_aspect("equal")
axL.set_xlabel("$x_1$"); axL.set_ylabel("$x_2$")

axR.scatter(Zw[0], Zw[1], s=8, color=MAPPED, alpha=0.4)
ex2, ey2 = cov_ellipse_xy(np.zeros(2), Sigma_Z, 2.0)
axR.plot(ex2, ey2, color=MAPPED, lw=1.8)
axR.set_title(r"Whitened: $\mathrm{Cov}=I$ (a circle)"); axR.set_aspect("equal")
axR.set_xlabel("$z_1$"); axR.set_ylabel("$z_2$")
plt.tight_layout()
print("Covariance of the whitened vector (should be the identity):")
print(np.round(Sigma_Z, 6))
```

The printed matrix is the identity to six decimals — the formula did exactly what
@eq-whitening promises.

::: {.callout-tip title="You might be asked"}
*"Is the whitening matrix unique?"* No, and this is a favorite follow-up. Any $W$ with
$W\boldsymbol\Sigma W^\top = I$ whitens, and there are infinitely many: if $W_0$ whitens,
so does $QW_0$ for **any** orthogonal matrix $Q$, since $(QW_0)\boldsymbol\Sigma(QW_0)^\top
= Q(W_0\boldsymbol\Sigma W_0^\top)Q^\top = QIQ^\top = QQ^\top = I$. Geometrically: once the
cloud is a perfect circle, you can rotate it freely and it stays a circle. The symmetric
choice $W=\boldsymbol\Sigma^{-1/2}$ (ZCA whitening) is the unique *symmetric* one and rotates
the data least; the choice $W=\Lambda^{-1/2}V^\top$ (PCA whitening) aligns the result with
the principal axes. Same covariance $I$, different rotation — exactly the non-uniqueness the
orthogonal $Q$ describes.
:::

# Worked examples {#sec-examples}

We run the two formulas on honest problems — and end on one near the edge of applicability.

## Example 1 — portfolio variance: combining correlated returns {#sec-portfolio}

The cleanest real use of @eq-quadratic-form is finance. Let $\mathbf X=(X_1,X_2,X_3)$ be the
random one-period returns of three assets, with mean vector $\boldsymbol\mu$ (expected
returns) and covariance matrix $\boldsymbol\Sigma$ (the risk model — variances on the
diagonal, return co-movements off it). A portfolio puts weight $w_i$ in asset $i$ (the $w_i$
sum to $1$), so its return is the *scalar* linear combination $R = \mathbf w^\top\mathbf X$.
The investor wants two numbers: the portfolio's expected return and its risk (variance).
Both are our formulas with $A=\mathbf w^\top$ a single row:
$$
E[R] = \mathbf w^\top\boldsymbol\mu, \qquad
\operatorname{Var}(R) = \mathbf w^\top\boldsymbol\Sigma\,\mathbf w .
$$

```{python}
#| label: fig-portfolio
#| fig-cap: "Portfolio risk from the covariance rule. Each grey point is a random weighting of three correlated assets; its expected return $\\mathbf w^\\top\\boldsymbol\\mu$ and risk $\\sqrt{\\mathbf w^\\top\\boldsymbol\\Sigma\\mathbf w}$ are computed entirely from the two formulas of this article. The red frontier traces the lowest risk attainable at each return -- the classic mean-variance efficient frontier, which exists *only because* correlation ($\\mathrm{Cov}<0$ between assets 1 and 3 here) lets a mix be less risky than its parts. The single asset markers show that diversification sits inside, not on, the individual assets."
rng = np.random.default_rng(20)
mu = np.array([0.08, 0.12, 0.10])                 # expected annual returns
sd = np.array([0.15, 0.25, 0.20])                 # annual volatilities
corr = np.array([[1.0,  0.30, -0.40],
                 [0.30, 1.0,   0.20],
                 [-0.40, 0.20, 1.0 ]])
Sigma = np.outer(sd, sd) * corr                   # covariance = D corr D

# random long-only portfolios (weights >= 0, sum to 1)
W = rng.dirichlet(np.ones(3), size=4000)
ret = W @ mu
risk = np.sqrt(np.einsum("ij,jk,ik->i", W, Sigma, W))   # w^T Sigma w per row

# crude efficient frontier: min risk in each return bucket
order = np.argsort(ret)
ret_s, risk_s = ret[order], risk[order]
bins = np.linspace(ret_s.min(), ret_s.max(), 40)
idx = np.digitize(ret_s, bins)
fr_ret, fr_risk = [], []
for b in range(1, len(bins) + 1):
    m = idx == b
    if m.any():
        fr_ret.append(ret_s[m].mean()); fr_risk.append(risk_s[m].min())

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(6.6, 4.4))
ax.scatter(risk, ret, s=6, color=GREY, alpha=0.35, label="random portfolios")
ax.plot(fr_risk, fr_ret, color=MAPPED, lw=2.4, label="efficient frontier")
for i, name in enumerate(["asset 1", "asset 2", "asset 3"]):
    ax.scatter([sd[i]], [mu[i]], color=CLOUD, s=80, zorder=5, edgecolor="white")
    ax.annotate(name, (sd[i], mu[i]), textcoords="offset points", xytext=(6, 4), fontsize=9)
ax.set_xlabel(r"risk  $\sqrt{\mathbf w^\top\Sigma\,\mathbf w}$")
ax.set_ylabel(r"expected return  $\mathbf w^\top\boldsymbol\mu$")
ax.set_title("Mean-variance frontier from $A\\Sigma A^\\top$")
ax.legend(loc="lower right", fontsize=9, framealpha=0.9)
plt.tight_layout()
```

The lesson is the off-diagonal: if the assets were uncorrelated, $\mathbf w^\top
\boldsymbol\Sigma\mathbf w$ would be just $\sum_i w_i^2\sigma_i^2$ and diversification would
only ever average risk. The *negative* covariance between assets 1 and 3 makes the
cross-terms subtract, so a mix can be **less risky than any single asset** — the bulge of the
frontier to the left of the asset markers. That entire phenomenon is the off-diagonal of
$\boldsymbol\Sigma$ acting through @eq-quadratic-form; it is invisible to the variances
alone. This is why $A\boldsymbol\Sigma A^\top$, and not a list of separate variances, is the
right object.

## Example 2 — error propagation: from polar to Cartesian coordinates {#sec-error-prop}

A second honest use, from the metrology that birthed the method. A surveyor measures a
point in **polar** coordinates: a range $r$ and a bearing $\theta$, each with measurement
noise. The downstream computation needs **Cartesian** coordinates $x=r\cos\theta$,
$y=r\sin\theta$. How does the measurement uncertainty propagate? The map is *not* linear —
but for small errors it is well approximated by its linear part (the **Jacobian** matrix of
first partial derivatives, the multivariable cousin of the single-variable derivative used
in [PROB-04]'s change of variables). Near the measured values $(r_0,\theta_0)$,
$$
\begin{pmatrix} \delta x \\ \delta y \end{pmatrix}
\approx
\underbrace{\begin{pmatrix} \cos\theta_0 & -r_0\sin\theta_0 \\ \sin\theta_0 & r_0\cos\theta_0 \end{pmatrix}}_{J}
\begin{pmatrix} \delta r \\ \delta\theta \end{pmatrix},
$$
so $\mathbf{(\delta x,\delta y)} \approx J\,\mathbf{(\delta r,\delta\theta)}$ — a linear map
with $A=J$. The covariance rule @eq-cov-rule then propagates the polar covariance into the
Cartesian one:
$$
\boldsymbol\Sigma_{xy} \approx J\,\boldsymbol\Sigma_{r\theta}\,J^\top .
$$
This *is* the propagation-of-error formula of every physics lab, derived as a one-line
special case of this article's theorem.

```{python}
#| label: fig-error-prop
#| fig-cap: "Error propagation through a coordinate change. **Left:** independent range and bearing errors -- the polar covariance is diagonal (an axis-aligned ellipse in the $r,\\theta$ plane). **Right:** mapped to Cartesian coordinates via $J\\Sigma J^\\top$, the uncertainty ellipse is *rotated and stretched*, and the once-independent errors are now correlated (the tilt). The dashed ellipse is computed from the formula; the points are a simulated sample for comparison. A larger bearing uncertainty would stretch the ellipse *along the arc*, the honest geometric meaning of angular error at range."
rng = np.random.default_rng(1)
r0, th0 = 50.0, np.deg2rad(35.0)
Sig_rt = np.array([[0.8 ** 2, 0.0],
                   [0.0, np.deg2rad(2.0) ** 2]])     # 0.8 m range SD, 2 deg bearing SD
J = np.array([[np.cos(th0), -r0 * np.sin(th0)],
              [np.sin(th0),  r0 * np.cos(th0)]])
Sig_xy = J @ Sig_rt @ J.T

# simulate to overlay
vals, vecs = np.linalg.eigh(Sig_rt)
Lr = vecs @ np.diag(np.sqrt(vals)) @ vecs.T
d_rt = Lr @ rng.normal(size=(2, 1200))
d_xy = J @ d_rt

fig, (axL, axR) = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(8.8, 4.0))
axL.scatter(d_rt[0], np.rad2deg(d_rt[1]), s=8, color=CLOUD, alpha=0.4)
# ellipse in (r, theta-in-degrees) space: scale the theta axis to degrees for display
ex, ey = cov_ellipse_xy(np.zeros(2), Sig_rt, 2.0)
axL.plot(ex, np.rad2deg(ey), color=CLOUD, ls="--", lw=1.8)
axL.set_title(r"Polar errors: independent")
axL.set_xlabel(r"$\delta r$ (m)"); axL.set_ylabel(r"$\delta\theta$ (deg)")

axR.scatter(d_xy[0], d_xy[1], s=8, color=MAPPED, alpha=0.4)
ex2, ey2 = cov_ellipse_xy(np.zeros(2), Sig_xy, 2.0)
axR.plot(ex2, ey2, color=MAPPED, ls="--", lw=1.8)
axR.set_title(r"Cartesian errors: $J\Sigma J^\top$ (correlated)")
axR.set_xlabel(r"$\delta x$ (m)"); axR.set_ylabel(r"$\delta y$ (m)"); axR.set_aspect("equal")
plt.tight_layout()
```

Two honest notes. First, this is the *approximate* application — the map is nonlinear and we
linearized; the covariance rule is exact only for the linear approximation, and the
approximation degrades as the errors grow. That is the boundary, and it leads directly into
the next example. Second, the propagated covariance is correlated even though the inputs were
independent — the Jacobian's rotation introduced the tilt, exactly as $A$ did in
@fig-ellipse-decomp.

## Example 3 (edge of applicability) — when only two moments is not enough {#sec-edge}

Here is the honest limit. The formulas @eq-mean-rule and @eq-cov-rule are *exactly* correct
for any $\mathbf X$ — but they describe only the **mean and the covariance**, and for a
non-Gaussian, non-linear, or heavy-tailed situation those two summaries can badly mislead
about the thing you actually care about. We show two failure shapes.

```{python}
#| label: fig-edge
#| fig-cap: "Where 'mean and covariance' stops being the whole story. **Left:** a strongly *nonlinear* map $\\,(x_1,x_2)\\mapsto(x_1,\\,x_1^2+x_2)$ turns a round Gaussian cloud into a curved boomerang; the covariance-rule ellipse (computed by linearizing at the mean, dashed) is a hopeless summary of a banana-shaped cloud -- the linear formula has no curvature to give. **Right:** a *heavy-tailed* input (a bivariate Student-$t$ with 2 degrees of freedom) has a covariance matrix that barely exists and is wildly unstable from sample to sample; the ellipse drawn from one sample's $\\Sigma$ (dashed) ignores the extreme points the tails routinely produce. In both regimes the two formulas are not *wrong*, they are *insufficient*."
rng = np.random.default_rng(4)

# Left: nonlinear map
mu = np.array([0.0, 0.0]); Sig = np.array([[1.0, 0.0], [0.0, 1.0]])
X = rng.normal(size=(2, 1500))
Yx = X[0]
Yy = X[0] ** 2 + X[1]
# linearized Jacobian at the mean (0,0): d(x1)=[1,0], d(x1^2+x2)=[2*x1, 1] -> at 0: [0,1]
Jlin = np.array([[1.0, 0.0], [0.0, 1.0]])
Sig_lin = Jlin @ Sig @ Jlin.T

# Right: heavy-tailed bivariate t (df=2) built as Gaussian / sqrt(chi2/df)
df = 2
G = rng.normal(size=(2, 1500))
chi = rng.chisquare(df, size=1500) / df
T = G / np.sqrt(chi)
Sig_t_sample = np.cov(T)

fig, (axL, axR) = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(8.8, 4.0))
axL.scatter(Yx, Yy, s=8, color=CLOUD, alpha=0.4)
ex, ey = cov_ellipse_xy(np.array([0.0, 1.0]), Sig_lin, 2.0)  # mean of x1^2 is 1
axL.plot(ex, ey, color=MAPPED, ls="--", lw=2.0)
axL.set_title("Nonlinear map: ellipse misses the curve")
axL.set_xlabel("$y_1$"); axL.set_ylabel("$y_2$")

axR.scatter(T[0], T[1], s=8, color=CLOUD, alpha=0.4)
ex2, ey2 = cov_ellipse_xy(np.zeros(2), Sig_t_sample, 2.0)
axR.plot(ex2, ey2, color=MAPPED, ls="--", lw=2.0)
axR.set_title("Heavy tails: $\\Sigma$ unstable, ignores outliers")
axR.set_xlabel("$z_1$"); axR.set_ylabel("$z_2$")
axR.set_xlim(-12, 12); axR.set_ylim(-12, 12)
plt.tight_layout()
```

The takeaway, stated plainly: **the two formulas are tools for the first two moments, and
they are exact for those — but the first two moments are a complete description only for the
Gaussian (and a faithful one for mild, nearly-linear, light-tailed situations).** When the
map is strongly nonlinear, linearizing and applying $J\boldsymbol\Sigma J^\top$ gives an
ellipse where the truth is a banana. When the input is heavy-tailed (a Student-$t$ with few
degrees of freedom, [PROB-15]), the covariance may barely exist and is so sample-unstable
that the ellipse is nearly meaningless. The honest practitioner move is to *check the
regime first*: is the map close to linear over the spread of the inputs, and does
$\mathbf X$ have light enough tails that $\boldsymbol\Sigma$ is a stable, representative
summary? If yes, these formulas are the right and complete tool. If no, you reach for
something that tracks more than two moments — full simulation (push samples through the
actual map, no linearization), or a model that does not lean on covariance at all.

# Failure modes and the edge of applicability {#sec-failure}

Collecting the honest boundaries, so the applicability call is one place.

::: {.callout-warning title="Pitfall"}
**The formulas are about moments, not distributions.** $E[A\mathbf X+\mathbf
b]=A\boldsymbol\mu+\mathbf b$ and $\operatorname{Cov}(A\mathbf X+\mathbf
b)=A\boldsymbol\Sigma A^\top$ are *unconditionally* true whenever the moments exist — no
independence, no normality needed. But they tell you only the mean and the covariance of the
output. They do **not** tell you its distribution unless you imported a closure fact (and the
only clean one is the Gaussian's, @thm-gaussian-closure). Confusing "I know the output's
covariance" with "I know the output's distribution" is the central trap.
:::

The three concrete boundaries:

- **Strong nonlinearity (@sec-error-prop, @sec-edge).** Applying $A\boldsymbol\Sigma
  A^\top$ to a nonlinear map requires linearizing (the Jacobian), and the result is only as
  good as that linear approximation. Over large input spreads it can be badly wrong. *Reach
  for:* Monte-Carlo propagation — push many samples through the exact map and measure the
  output spread directly.
- **Heavy tails / non-existent moments (@sec-edge).** If $\boldsymbol\Sigma$ barely exists
  (Student-$t$ with low df, Cauchy with no variance at all — the divergent case flagged in
  [PROB-05]), the formula's inputs are unstable or undefined. *Reach for:* robust scatter
  estimates, or quantile-based descriptions of spread.
- **Degeneracy / rank-deficiency (@sec-psd).** If $A$ collapses a dimension (rank-deficient)
  or $\boldsymbol\Sigma$ is singular, the output covariance is singular and the cloud lives
  on a lower-dimensional subspace — and $\boldsymbol\Sigma^{-1/2}$ for whitening does not
  exist. *Reach for:* the pseudo-inverse / a reduced-rank treatment (Geometry of Data).

::: {.callout-tip title="You might be asked"}
*"When is the linear-transform machinery the right tool, and when not?"* The summary to be
able to recite:

- **Use it** whenever you need the mean or covariance of a *linear* (or locally-linear)
  combination of random quantities whose first two moments are well-defined and stable:
  portfolio risk, error propagation in measurement, the sampling covariance of a linear
  estimator like $\hat{\boldsymbol\beta}$ in [REGR-06], any preprocessing that decorrelates
  features (whitening, GLS). For the Gaussian it gives the *entire* output distribution.
- **Do not lean on it** when the map is strongly nonlinear over the input spread (linearize
  only if the curvature is mild, else simulate), when the input is heavy-tailed enough that
  $\boldsymbol\Sigma$ is unstable or undefined, or when you need a *full distribution* of a
  non-Gaussian output (the moments alone will not give it).
- **The deepest caveat:** $A\boldsymbol\Sigma A^\top$ summarizes spread, not shape — it
  cannot see skew, multimodality, or fat tails, and treating it as if it does is how
  "two-moment" risk models miss the events that matter.
:::

# How you would actually compute this {#sec-implementation}

The math is primary; this is the secondary, practical note. You never form
$A\boldsymbol\Sigma A^\top$ by the double sum. In any numerical stack it is two matrix
multiplies — `A @ Sigma @ A.T` in NumPy — and the mean is `A @ mu + b`. For *whitening*, do
**not** literally invert $\boldsymbol\Sigma$ and take a square root by eigendecomposition in
performance code; the numerically preferred route is a **Cholesky factorization**
$\boldsymbol\Sigma = LL^\top$ with $L$ lower-triangular (a cheap, stable factorization),
after which $W=L^{-1}$ whitens — because $L^{-1}\boldsymbol\Sigma(L^{-1})^\top =
L^{-1}LL^\top L^{-\top} = I$ — and triangular solves avoid forming an explicit inverse. The
symmetric $\boldsymbol\Sigma^{-1/2}$ of @sec-whitening is the choice when you specifically
want the *symmetric* (minimal-rotation) whitening; the eigendecomposition that defines it is
also what the **Geometry of Data** track uses for PCA, tying whitening and principal
components to the same factorization. Whichever route, the *answer* is the one this article
derived: mean $A\boldsymbol\mu+\mathbf b$, covariance $A\boldsymbol\Sigma A^\top$.

# Connections and where to go next {#sec-connections}

::: {.callout-important title="Where you are in the track"}
You arrived from **[PROB-17 *Random Vectors, Mean Vectors, and Covariance Matrices*]**,
which built the three objects — the random vector, its mean vector $\boldsymbol\mu$, and its
covariance matrix $\boldsymbol\Sigma$ — and established that $\boldsymbol\Sigma$ is symmetric
and positive semidefinite via $\mathbf a^\top\boldsymbol\Sigma\mathbf a =
\operatorname{Var}(\mathbf a^\top\mathbf X)\ge 0$. This article took those objects and asked
what a linear map does to them, deriving the mean rule and the sandwich covariance rule from
linearity of expectation alone. From here Movement 4 branches:

- **[PROB-19 *Chebyshev's Inequality*]** — with the variance of a linear combination
  ($\mathbf a^\top\boldsymbol\Sigma\mathbf a$) in hand, we bound how much probability can sit
  far from the mean, and sketch why averages concentrate.
- **[PROB-20 *Moment Generating and Characteristic Functions*]** — the transform tools that
  *prove* the Gaussian closure we imported in @thm-gaussian-closure, and that turn sums of
  independents into products.

And the cross-track payoffs this article was built to deliver, by name:

- **[REGR-06]** derives the sampling covariance $\operatorname{Var}(\hat{\boldsymbol\beta}) =
  \sigma^2(X^\top X)^{-1}$ by writing $\hat{\boldsymbol\beta}=(X^\top X)^{-1}X^\top\mathbf y$
  as a linear transform $A\mathbf y$ of the response and applying @eq-cov-rule — *this exact
  identity*.
- **[REGR-11]** (generalized least squares) whitens correlated errors with the transform of
  @sec-whitening, turning a correlated problem back into an ordinary-least-squares one.
- **Geometry of Data** owns the eigendecomposition, positive-definiteness theory, and matrix
  square root we imported; the covariance matrix is the bridge — its probabilistic meaning
  here, its spectral analysis (and PCA) there.
:::

# Going deeper {#sec-deeper}

- **The propagation-of-error lineage.** Gauss's *Theoria combinationis observationum
  erroribus minimis obnoxiae* (1823) is the origin of the variance-propagation rule that
  @sec-error-prop is a special case of; every modern metrology guide (e.g. the *GUM*, the
  Guide to the Expression of Uncertainty in Measurement) restates $J\boldsymbol\Sigma
  J^\top$ as its core formula.
- **Mean-variance portfolio theory.** Harry Markowitz, "Portfolio Selection," *Journal of
  Finance* 7 (1952): the efficient frontier of @sec-portfolio is exactly $\min \mathbf
  w^\top\boldsymbol\Sigma\mathbf w$ subject to a target return — a quadratic form in the
  covariance matrix, optimized.
- **Whitening and its cousins.** The comparison of ZCA whitening
  ($\boldsymbol\Sigma^{-1/2}$, symmetric, minimal rotation) and PCA whitening
  ($\Lambda^{-1/2}V^\top$) is laid out in Kessy, Lewin & Strimmer, "Optimal Whitening and
  Decorrelation," *The American Statistician* 72 (2018) — the cleanest modern reference for
  the non-uniqueness discussed in @sec-whitening.
- **Open threads this article deliberately deferred.** *Why is the Gaussian the one family
  closed under linear maps?* — the characteristic-function argument is completed in
  [PROB-20]. *What does the eigenstructure of $\boldsymbol\Sigma$ mean, and how does the
  matrix square root actually get computed?* — Geometry of Data. *What if the "map" is the
  sample mean of many vectors — where does its covariance go as $n\to\infty$?* — that is the
  multivariate Central Limit Theorem, owned by the Statistical Inference track, with the
  shrink $\boldsymbol\Sigma/n$ falling straight out of @eq-cov-rule.
